title_en: Methodology and Structure of Nihayat al-Muhtaj
content_en:
Nihayat al-Muhtaj ila Sharh al-Minhaj — The Endpoint of the One in Need, a Commentary on al-Minhaj — is ar-Ramli's monumental explanation of an-Nawawi's Minhaj at-Talibin, the compact legal manual that became the central text of the Shafi'i curriculum. Like Tuhfat al-Muhtaj by Ibn Hajar al-Haytami, which is also a commentary on the Minhaj, the Nihaya represents the culmination of the Shafi'i commentarial tradition on this core text. The two works are studied side by side in advanced Shafi'i education.

Ar-Ramli's methodology is characterized by deep engagement with the positions of his predecessors within the Shafi'i tradition, particularly with the Rawdat at-Talibin and al-Majmu of an-Nawawi, the Mughni al-Muhtaj of al-Khatib ash-Shirbini, and — in constant dialogue — the Tuhfa of Ibn Hajar al-Haytami. Where ar-Ramli and Ibn Hajar al-Haytami disagree, he states his position clearly and argues for it from the textual sources. These disagreements between the two great authorities of the late Shafi'i school created two branches of the tradition — the Egyptian school following ar-Ramli and the Hijazi school following Ibn Hajar — a division that persists to the present day.

Structurally, the Nihaya follows the Minhaj chapter by chapter, expanding each section with additional detail, citing the opinions of earlier scholars, resolving ambiguities in the base text, and adding cases that an-Nawawi's concise manual did not address. The work runs to eight volumes in the standard edition. Ar-Ramli's prose is precise and often terse, reflecting his training in a tradition that valued economy of expression alongside comprehensiveness of coverage.

A notable feature of the Nihaya is its treatment of the hashiyas produced on the Minhaj by earlier scholars. Ar-Ramli evaluates these marginal commentaries, incorporating what he considers sound and rejecting what he considers mistaken. This critical engagement with the secondary literature shows his command of the entire tradition and his willingness to exercise independent judgment within it.
